Retail Pricing Analysis · Hass Avocado Board · 2015–2018

Hass avocado prices: what drives them, can we forecast them, and what happens if we change them?

A decision-grade read on 18,249 weekly multi-outlet retail records, structured to the McKinsey answer-first standard. Three questions, three methods: diagnostics for the drivers, a forward forecast for planning, and a causal (instrumental-variables) study for the pricing lever.

Executive Summary · Situation → Complication → Resolution

SituationHass avocados sold across 50+ US markets in two lines — conventional and organic — averaged $1.41 per avocado over 2015–2018.
ComplicationPer-unit price swings from $0.44 to $3.25. Leadership needs three things: to know what drives the swing, to forecast price for planning, and to know whether raising price would grow revenue.
ResolutionThe swing is systematic and largely predictable — but the pricing lever backfires: demand is price-elastic, so higher prices shrink revenue.
Price is structured by four learnable drivers (type, geography, season, supply), which a forward model turns into a ±$0.11 one-week-ahead forecast — but a causal IV study shows avocado demand is price-elastic (−1.43), meaning a 10% price rise cuts volume ~14% and total revenue ~4%. Grow through mix and forecasting, not headline price increases.
  1. Type sets the baseline. Organic commands a +42.8% premium ($0.50/avocado) — the largest, most reliable price gap in the data (Cohen's d = 1.56).
  2. Geography and season layer on. City alone explains 17.5% of price variance (Houston $0.98 → Hartford $1.80), and prices follow a repeatable curve — winter trough, autumn peak — while rising supply pushes price down (ρ = −0.61).
  3. Price is forecastable. A one-week-ahead model predicts price within ±$0.11 (R² = 0.78); a SARIMAX model forecasts the stable organic line 26 weeks out at 2.2% error, though the volatile conventional line is far harder.
  4. The pricing lever is causal — and negative. Instrumenting for supply/demand simultaneity, own-price elasticity is −1.43 (elastic). Raising price loses revenue; the growth levers are product mix, region, and timing.
Recommendation The category team should (1) run type- and region-segmented weekly price forecasts (±$0.11 band) for demand and margin planning; (2) grow revenue through organic mix and premium-market allocation, not broad price increases, given elastic demand; and (3) time promotions to the known demand events (Super Bowl, Cinco de Mayo) rather than discounting into already-high-demand weeks.
+42.8%
Organic price premium over conventional
2.0×
Cheapest→priciest city spread ($0.98→$1.80)
±$0.11
One-week-ahead forecast error (MAE, R²=0.78)
−1.43
Causal price elasticity of demand (IV) — elastic

Part I · Diagnostic (Path A)

What drives the price?

Key Line 01

Type sets the baseline: organic costs 43% more than conventional

Product type is the sharpest fault line in the data. Conventional avocados cluster around a $1.13 median, organic around $1.63 — a $0.50 (42.8%) premium. The effect size (Cohen's d = 1.56) is large: the two price distributions barely overlap.

So what: Type is the first cut for any pricing or forecasting model — pooling the lines blurs a $0.50 structural gap.

Key Line 02a

Geography adds a ~2× spread across cities

Median price ranges from $0.98 in Houston to $1.80 in Hartford–Springfield across the 43 city markets (aggregate regions excluded). ANOVA confirms the gap is real (F = 73, p ≈ 0); region alone explains 17.5% of price variance (η² = 0.175).

Key Line 02b

Season and supply move price predictably

Prices trough in Jan–Feb, peak in Sep–Oct, and fall as volume rises (Spearman ρ = −0.61) — high-volume weeks are low-price weeks. The 2017 peak year ($1.52 vs. ~$1.35 elsewhere) reflects a documented supply shortfall.

So what: Season and expected volume are the time-varying inputs a forecast needs — and both are known or estimable ahead of time, which is what makes prediction feasible.

Part II · Predictive (Path B)

Can we forecast it?

Key Line 03 · Forward forecast

A one-week-ahead model predicts price within ±$0.11 — using only past data

The earlier concurrent model used same-week volume (a diagnostic "what price fits these conditions" view). To make an honest operational forecast, we rebuilt it to use only information known before the target week — lagged prices, rolling averages, lagged volume, a promo proxy (last week's volume surge), and a calendar of known-ahead demand events (Super Bowl, Cinco de Mayo, Thanksgiving, July 4th, Christmas). Trained on the earliest 80% of weeks, tested on the most recent 20%.

Forward model (1-week-ahead)RMSE ($)MAE ($)R²MAPE
Seasonal-naive (price 52w ago)0.3490.268−0.1318.7%
Naive (last week's price)0.1700.1180.739.1%
Gradient boosting (no events)0.1570.1130.778.8%
Gradient boosting (+ events/promo) ★0.1530.1110.788.6%
So what: The forward model beats a seasonal-naive baseline by 56% and forecasts next week within ±$0.11 — good enough for weekly demand/margin planning. Adding the event calendar cuts error a further 2.7%; the honest caveat is that at a one-week horizon, last week's price already carries most of the signal.

Key Line 03 · Multi-week horizon

Forecasting further out works for organic, but the volatile conventional line resists it

For genuine multi-week planning we fit a classical SARIMAX model (Fourier annual seasonality + event regressors) and forecast 26 weeks ahead with prediction intervals. The result is a study in honesty about forecast difficulty:

So what: The stable, strongly-seasonal organic series forecasts beautifully 6 months out (2.2% error). The conventional series — hit by the 2017 supply shock — is genuinely hard, and a naive seasonal benchmark is competitive. Use multi-week forecasts for organic planning; for conventional, rely on the short-horizon model and refresh weekly.

Part III · Causal (Path C)

What happens if we change the price?

Key Line 04 · Causal study

Demand is price-elastic (−1.43): raising price loses revenue

The diagnostics (Part I) show price and volume move together — but that is a correlation, and it cannot answer "what if we raise price?" Price and quantity are set jointly by supply and demand, so a naive regression is confounded: a Super Bowl demand spike lifts both price and volume, biasing the elasticity toward zero. This is a textbook instrumental-variables problem.

Supply shocks ──▶ Price ──▶ Quantity ← the causal path we want Demand shocks ──▶ Price Demand shocks ──▶ Quantity ← back-door confounding (biases OLS) Instrument: other-markets' price ──▶ local Price ──▶ Quantity (excludes demand)

We instrument each market's price with the average price of the same avocado type in all other city markets that week — a supply/cost shifter that moves local price but (assumed) affects local demand only through price. Region, month, and year fixed effects absorb structural and seasonal confounders. The instrument is very strong (first-stage F = 350, far above the F > 10 rule).

EstimatorElasticity95% CIReading
OLS (confounded)−1.28[−1.42, −1.14]biased toward 0
IV / 2SLS (causal) ★−1.43[−1.61, −1.25]elastic
So what: With elasticity −1.43, a 10% price increase cuts volume ~14% and total revenue ~4% (revenue change ≈ (1 + elasticity) × price change). The lever for revenue growth is therefore not headline price — it is shifting mix toward organic, allocating supply to premium markets, and protecting volume. This is a causal claim under a stated exclusion assumption, not a correlation.

Appendix — Method, data & limitations

Pipeline

Executed against the project's DOCS/STRUCTURE.md standard: Stage 1 ingestion (immutable raw copy, schema checks) → Stage 2 cleaning (0 nulls, 0 duplicates, rules price>0, volume≥0) → Stage 3 hypothesis-driven EDA → Path A stats (effect sizes with every p-value) → Path B concurrent + forward + SARIMAX forecasting → Path C IV causal study. Reproducible: seed 42, relative paths, code in src/ (5 scripts).

Data

18,249 weekly multi-outlet retail scan records (grocery, mass, club, drug, dollar, military), Jan 2015 – Mar 2018. Average Price is per single avocado (even in bags). PLU 4046 / 4225 / 4770 = small / large / xlarge Hass; greenskins excluded. The source header says "2018" but records span 2015–2018.

Forecasting detail

Forward model: HistGradientBoosting on lagged prices (1/2/4/52w), rolling means (4/12w), price momentum, lagged volume, a promo proxy (last week's volume vs. its trailing-12w median), cyclical month, year, trend, region/type, and five event flags. Temporal split at 2017-10-15. SARIMAX: order (2,0,2) with constant, Fourier (K=3, period 52.18) + Super Bowl / Cinco de Mayo regressors, 26-week hold-out, per type on the Total-US series.

Causal detail

IV2SLS (linearmodels) on 14,534 city-level rows. Outcome log(volume), endogenous regressor log(price), instrument = leave-one-out mean log-price across other markets (same type & week). Region + month + year + type fixed effects; standard errors clustered by region.

Limitations & honest caveats.
  • Causal claim rests on the exclusion restriction. The IV assumes other-markets' prices affect local demand only through local price. Nationally-correlated demand shocks (e.g., a Super Bowl spike everywhere at once) would violate it; month/year fixed effects mitigate but do not eliminate this. Treat −1.43 as a well-identified estimate under a stated, testable-in-part assumption — not incontrovertible truth.
  • Elasticity is an average. It pools types, regions, and price ranges; organic vs. conventional and premium vs. value markets likely differ. A follow-up should estimate heterogeneous elasticities (CATE) before setting segment-specific prices.
  • Forward forecast is one-week-ahead. Multi-week forecasts require recursive projection and are materially less accurate for the volatile conventional line (see SARIMAX).
  • No true promotion field. The dataset has no promo flag; we proxy it with a lagged volume surge and known demand events. A real promotion calendar would improve both forecast and elasticity work.
  • Unexplained variance. Local weather, store mix, and idiosyncratic promotions remain uncaptured and bound achievable accuracy.

Next steps

(1) Estimate heterogeneous elasticities by type × region (causal ML / CATE) to guide segment-level pricing; (2) integrate a real promotion calendar; (3) operationalize the forward model as a weekly type×region price-expectation table with the ±$0.11 band; (4) validate the elasticity with a small geo-based price experiment (the gold-standard Path-C design).

Prepared to the McKinsey communication standard (Pyramid · MECE · SCR · action titles · answer-first). Three analytical paths — diagnostic (A), predictive (B), causal (C) — per DOCS/STRUCTURE.md. Data: Hass Avocado Board multi-outlet retail scan, 2015–2018. Fully reproducible from src/.
